case_name: Watts v North Bristol NHS Trust [2022] EWHC 2048 (QB) (29 July 2022)

summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: Mr Katsimihas conducted a pre-operative consultation with Mr Watts on 27 March 2014 and carried out spinal fusion surgery on 29 April 2014.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Judge found in Mr Watts' favour on the first issue. When proposing spinal fusion, Mr Katsimihas failed in his duty to identify the reasonable options together with their advantages and disadvantages.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The expert witnesses agreed that the prospect of improving back pain with microdiscectomy was 30% whilst with fusion it was 60%.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witnesses

evidence_index.text: The Judge decided that he could not be satisfied on the balance of probabilities that Mr Watts would have chosen microdiscectomy.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Mr Watts, born in 1973, was a self-employed landscape gardener.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Harding recommended microforaminotomy, which I am told is effectively the same as microdiscectomy.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The Judge's conclusion was not clearly wrong, and ground 1 therefore fails.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Judge, noting that both types of operation were reasonable options, considered whether any other evidence supported the Appellant's assertion.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Mr Webb in his original report criticised both the decision to offer fusion and the way in which it was carried out.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The failure of ground 1 means that the appeal must be dismissed.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Severe back and left leg pain
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mr Watts experienced intermittent back pain from his teens, with episodes of left leg sciatica and low back pain since 2008. Severe pain and numbness in the left leg occurred on 20 April 2013, leading to an A&E visit. An MRI in May 2013 showed nerve impingement between L5 and S1 due to a prolapsed disc.

focused_exam.neurological: Weakness, numbness, and severe back pain noted

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Chronic back and leg pain with nerve compression
critical_findings: Nerve impingement between L5 and S1

requestables.name: MRI Lumbar Spine
requestables.description: MRI to assess nerve impingement and disc status

reveal.result_summary: Impingement of the nerve between L5 and S1 due to a prolapsed disc

requestables.name: Pre-operative Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with Mr Katsimihas regarding surgical options

reveal.result_summary: Fusion recommended; microdiscectomy not sufficiently discussed
reveal.decision_impact: Led to decision for spinal fusion

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with chronic back and leg pain

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with chronic back and leg pain considering surgical options
decision_points.prompt: What surgical procedure should be recommended?

options.clinical_reasoning: Addressed tilt and instability but not fully informed consent

options.description: Discuss both spinal fusion and microdiscectomy with pros and cons
options.clinical_reasoning: Would have provided informed consent and options

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant recommended spinal fusion without adequate discussion of alternatives
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed fusion was appropriate due to tilt

expected_action_court.description: Should have discussed microdiscectomy as a reasonable alternative
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Informed consent requires discussion of reasonable options

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failed to provide informed consent by not discussing microdiscectomy
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Both procedures should have been discussed with pros and cons

patient_outcome.description: Patient left with worse pain and limited mobility post-surgery

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to obtain informed consent
Causation not established for better outcome with microdiscectomy

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to discuss microdiscectomy as an option
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Microdiscectomy could have been considered if informed consent was obtained

tests_performed.result: Nerve impingement between L5 and S1

court_findings.finding: Breach of duty in failing to obtain informed consent
